summary: A biographical overview of Thomas Edison's career, highlighting his pioneering of the industrial research laboratory, his focus on practical and profitable inventions, and his systems-level approach to electrification.

principles.statement: Pursue only inventions that are practical and profitable.
principles.rationale: To sustain the high costs of running a research laboratory and ensure the inventions actually reach and benefit society.
principles.source_quote: His method of raising money was to pursue only the inventions that were both 'practical and profitable.'

principles.statement: Focus on practical results rather than theoretical knowledge.
principles.rationale: Edison disdained the career of a 'pure scientist', preferring to systematically test materials and build things that worked in the real world.
principles.source_quote: his systematic approach to inventing focused on practical results rather than theoretical knowledge.

principles.statement: Create whole systems, not just isolated pieces.
principles.rationale: For an invention to be commercially viable and widely adopted, the entire infrastructure required to support it must also be built.
principles.source_quote: instead of just fabricating pieces of the electric puzzle, it was Edison’s intention to create a whole system, from electrical generation and distribution to the end products for home and business use.

frameworks.name: The Invention Factory
frameworks.when_to_apply: When aiming to produce marketable products more productively than a lone inventor could achieve.
steps: Generate initial ideas for practical products.
Assign dedicated teams of researchers ('muckers') to work on specific projects.
Systematically test materials and iterate until a viable product is found.
Develop the surrounding systems required to commercialize the product.
frameworks.source_quote: Edison’s system was to come up with ideas and assign teams of researchers to work on projects, whom he referred to as 'muckers.'

mental_models.name: Whole System Design
mental_models.description: Approaching a new technology not as an isolated device, but as an entire ecosystem that includes generation, distribution, and end-use products.
mental_models.example: Instead of just inventing the light bulb, Edison founded the Edison Illuminating Company, built the Pearl Street Power Station, and laid electrical conduits to deliver the necessary power.

heuristics: Only pursue inventions that are both practical and profitable.

signature_quotes.context: Edison's criteria for deciding which inventions to pursue in order to fund his laboratory.

anti_patterns.description: Acting as a 'pure scientist' focused solely on theoretical knowledge.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails to produce practical, marketable results that can be commercialized to fund further research.

anti_patterns.description: Using Alternating Current (AC) for electrical distribution.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Edison believed AC was inherently dangerous due to its high voltages and tendency to spark, leading to fatal accidents.
